audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/msgvault-bin.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-_lbo24ss...

Analyzing msgvault-bin AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-_lbo24ss) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard source entries that fetch files from the official GitHub repository of the project using HTTPS URLs. The `source` array retrieves the README and LICENSE files directly from the tagged release, and the binary tarballs for x86_64 and aarch64 are downloaded from the project's GitHub releases page. All URLs are properly templated using trusted variables (`_urlraw`, `url`) that resolve to the legitimate upstream source.

No command substitution, function execution, or unsafe code is present in the global scope or in `pkgver()`. The `pkgver` is hard-coded and does not execute any external commands or downloads during sourcing. The use of `sha256sums` for all sources — including architecture-specific binaries — ensures integrity validation. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, dynamic code evaluation, or remote payload execution during the sourcing phase.

Since `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` only sources the PKGBUILD and fetches sources, and because all operations at this stage are limited to verified downloads from the project's own upstream, the risk is minimal and consistent with standard AUR packaging practices. No malicious behavior is triggered during this phase.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: Standard, verified source downloads only
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe: Standard, verified source downloads only[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
[0/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD, LICENSE-0.16.0...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD file defines a standard binary package build process for the `msgvault-bin` package. It retrieves pre-built binaries and associated documentation (README and LICENSE) from the official GitHub repository of the project, using HTTPS URLs that point directly to tagged releases. The sources are properly scoped to the declared upstream (`https://github.com/wesm/msgvault`), and the integrity of each downloaded file is verified using hardcoded SHA-256 checksums, including architecture-specific binaries for x86_64 and aarch64.

The `package()` function performs only safe operations: it copies the pre-compiled binary to `/usr/bin`, and installs documentation and license files into standard FHS-compliant locations under `/usr/share/doc` and `/usr/share/licenses`. No runtime network requests, code execution, or dynamic downloading occurs during installation. There are no uses of `eval`, `base64`, `curl|bash`, or other dangerous patterns. All actions align with standard AUR packaging practices for a `-bin` package distributing upstream binaries.

The maintainer metadata and licensing are clearly stated, and dependencies are minimal and appropriate for a Rust-compiled binary (`glibc`, `libgcc`, `libstdc++`). No suspicious file operations, obfuscation, or post-install hooks that could enable backdoors or data exfiltration are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package with verified upstream sources
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing LICENSE-0.16.0...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard binary package with verified upstream sources
LLM auditresponse for LICENSE-0.16.0:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file provided is a standard MIT License text, which includes a copyright notice and standard permissive licensing terms. It contains no executable code, scripts, or commands, and therefore poses no security risk. The content is plain legal text describing the licensing conditions under which the software may be used, modified, and distributed.

There are no network operations, file manipulations, or system calls present, as this is purely a license file. The escaping of quotation marks (e.g., &quot;) is a result of XML encoding in the file delivery context and does not indicate obfuscation. Such files are routinely included in software distributions and AUR packages to comply with license requirements.

This file aligns with standard packaging practices and presents no evidence of malicious intent or supply chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
License file, no executable code or threats.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `.nvchecker.toml` file is a configuration file used by `nvchecker` to monitor upstream releases of software packages. In this case, it is configured to track the latest release of the `msgvault` project from the GitHub repository `wesm/msgvault`, with version tags prefixed by "v". This is a standard and expected use of nvchecker, commonly found in AUR packages to automate version updates.

There are no executable commands, network operations beyond the intended version checking, or suspicious behaviors such as data exfiltration, use of obfuscated code, or unauthorized file modifications. The file simply defines a source and tracking method for the package version. It does not perform any downloads, builds, or system changes on its own. Its purpose is informational and limited to version monitoring.

While the file could theoretically be misused if processed by an unexpected or compromised tool, in isolation it contains only declarative configuration and no active malicious logic. It adheres to typical AUR maintainer practices for version tracking.
</details>
<summary>
Standard version-tracking config, no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing README-0.16.0.md...
+ Reviewed .nvchecker.toml. Status: SAFE -- Standard version-tracking config, no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for README-0.16.0.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a README, not a script or executable code. It contains documentation for the `msgvault` tool, including installation instructions, feature descriptions, and usage examples. The content promotes standard and transparent installation methods such as downloading from GitHub Releases, building from source, or installing via Conda-Forge.

The installation scripts referenced (`install.sh` and `install.ps1`) are hosted over HTTPS from the official domain `msgvault.io`, and the README encourages users to review them before execution. These scripts are not embedded in the README itself, and their mentioned behavior—detecting OS, downloading binaries, and verifying checksums—is consistent with legitimate package installers.

No obfuscated code, suspicious network requests, or unauthorized system modifications are present in the document. The file does not execute any commands; it only describes how users can install and use the software. All described functionality aligns with the stated purpose of archiving and searching email locally.
</details>
<summary>
Documentation only; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>
